title: Lease Accounting
description:
Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS 116 vs IFRS 16 vs US GAAP ASC
            842

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Focuses on whether risks and rewards are transferred; operating vs finance distinction is central.
Most leases go on balance sheet using right-of-use (ROU) model; lessee recognizes ROU asset and lease liability.
Same ROU model as Ind AS.
Most leases also go on balance sheet, but retains dual classification for lessee.
From off-balance-sheet rental expense to asset + liability recognition.
Operating leases usually expensed straight-line; finance leases capitalized by substance.
Single lessee model for most leases.
Dual model: finance lease vs operating lease classification remains.
From two-way classification to a more asset-liability-centric model.
Operating leases often stayed off balance sheet, except finance leases.
Recognize ROU asset and lease liability at commencement for most leases.
Recognize ROU asset and lease liability for most leases, but P&L differs by classification.
Hidden commitments become visible on the balance sheet.
Finance lease assets/liab at lower of fair value and PV of minimum lease payments.
Lease liab = PV of payments; ROU asset = liab adjusted for prepayments, incentives, initial direct costs.
Same initial measurement logic, with classification affecting later expense pattern.
From fixed capitalization rules to discounted PV of lease cash flows.
Operating rentals expensed straight-line; finance lease liability reduced over time.
Interest expense on liability + depreciation of ROU asset.
Finance: interest + amortization; Operating: straight-line lease expense with separate ROU/liab.
Expense recognition depends on economic use of right-to-use.
Straight-line rent for operating, front-loaded interest for finance leases.
Front-loaded total expense due to interest + depreciation, except short-term/low-value relief.
Operating lease expense usually straight-line; finance lease expense front-loaded.
Timing of expense is much more explicit and economically linked.
Limited comparable old GAAP concept. Not central.
Short-term (12m or less) and low-value assets may be expensed instead of capitalized.
Similar short-term exemption. No equivalent broad low-value exemption.
Removes immaterial leases from balance sheet to reduce noise.
Based on contract/enforceable period, but less formalized.
Includes non-cancellable period + extension/termination options if reasonably certain.
Forces analysts to model options, not just legal minimum term.
Included in liab only if based on index/rate or fixed in substance. Remeasured when index changes.
For operating leases, liab generally not remeasured for CPI changes (expensed as incurred).
Variable economics separated; Key difference between IFRS & US GAAP.
Depends on whether new lease or reassessment is triggered.
Similar, but classification effects under US GAAP can differ.
Focus shift from form to reassessment of lease economics.
Generally mirrors transfer of risks and rewards.
Lessor keeps classification model: finance vs operating lease.
Lessee changed dramatically; lessor changed less.
Limited older guidance; substance over form.
Specific accounting; gains recognized only to extent of transferred rights.
Similar, but detailed US guidance may differ in form.
Transactions judged on control transfer and retained rights.
Operating Lease Classified as operating. Lease rentals expensed in P&L on straight-line basis (12.00 Cr / year). No right-of-use asset or lease liability recognized on balance sheet.
Capitalization Model Single lessee model. Capitalizes lease into Right-of-Use (ROU) asset (45.47 Cr) and Lease Liability (45.47 Cr). Produces a front-loaded expense pattern.
Capitalization Model Single lessee model. Fully aligned with Ind AS 116. Capitalized ROU asset and lease liability, with front-loaded depreciation and interest expense.
Operating Class On-balance sheet operating lease. Recognizes ROU asset and lease liability. However, lease expense is recognized straight-line (12.00 Cr / year).

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - Year 1

tableData: Higher CFO under Ind AS/IFRS due to reclassification of principal
Principal shown as financing outflow under Ind AS/IFRS
Total cash drain is identical across all frameworks
Operating lease now treated as fixed charge

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: Higher EBITDA but lower Net Income in early lease years
Operating lease rent is replaced by ROU depreciation and interest. Total expense is front-loaded.
Operating cash flows look stronger, but bottom line is penalized initially despite no economic change.
Assess EBITDA excluding lease impact for historical comparability.
Operating leases are capitalized on the balance sheet as Lease Liabilities.
Leverage ratios (e.g. Debt/Equity) appear to deteriorate significantly.
Distinguish between true interest-bearing debt and operational lease liabilities when evaluating covenants.
Higher Operating Cash Flow (CFO), Lower Financing CF (CFF)
Lease principal payments were moved from CFO under AS 19 to CFF under Ind AS 116 / IFRS 16.
Core operating generation appears artificially enhanced vs past periods.
Deduct lease principal payments from CFO to calculate true Free Cash Flow.
Recognition of ROU asset expands the total asset base denominator.
Asset turnover drops, making capital efficiency look worse mechanically.
Normalize ROA by using pre-Ind AS 116 asset base or adjusting net income.
Deduct actual cash rent paid to normalize.
Depression in early years, elevation in later years
Adjust interest to reflect straight-line economic cost.
Rep. 8.00 Cr vs Norm. 10.00 Cr in Yr 1
Strip out lease liabilities for traditional debt covenants.
Deduct lease principal & interest payments from CFO.
Exclude lease liabilities for classical debt covenants.
A spike is a reporting illusion; real debt didn't change unless new leases were signed.
Subtract right-of-use asset depreciation or actual lease payments.
EBITDA is artificially boosted because rent is shifted below the EBITDA line.
Remove ROU Assets from the asset base.
The asset base expanded mechanically, making capital efficiency look worse initially.
Exclude the implied lease interest expense.
Interest expense includes a non-cash lease interest component; true cash-interest coverage is stronger.
"Is the company taking on more debt?"
Total debt on BS surged suddenly without any new operational borrowing.
No. Operating leases were simply pushed onto the balance sheet.
Check Notes to Accounts for Lease Liabilities vs Traditional Bank Debt.
EBITDA margin jumped by ~10-15% this year.
No. Rent expense was removed from EBITDA and replaced by depreciation and interest below the line.
Compare 'Pre-Ind AS 116 EBITDA' or deduct cash rent paid.
CFO is much higher than last year.
No. The principal portion of rent is now a Financing outflow, inflating Operating flows artificially.
Look at Cash CFF outflow for "Principal portion of lease payments".
Net income is lower despite identical business operations.
Early years of a lease term have higher interest expense, front-loading the P&L hit compared to straight-line rent.
ROU Amortization schedule showing front-loaded total expense.
Panic sell; fear of bankruptcy or rate risk.
Strip out lease liabilities; traditional debt is 0.
Subtract actual rent paid to find normalized operating leverage.
Algorithmic buy based on cash generation.
Deduct financing lease outflows to find FCF.
Management is trying to provide comparable underlying performance, rather than hiding behind the standard's artificial boost.
Bragging about sudden CFO growth without mentioning leases
Attempting to pass off a standard-driven geography shift (CFO to CFF) as operational improvement.
Refocusing debt covenants to exclude lease liabilities
Ensuring operational covenants aren't triggered by accounting changes. Standard corporate treasury practice.

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Spike in 'short-term lease' expenses kept off balance sheet.
Ensure management isn't structuring leases to <12 months just to evade capitalization.
Unusually high discount rates applied to lease liabilities.
Compare to actual borrowing rates. High rates artificially shrink the reported lease liability.
Aggressive sale-and-leaseback transactions with inflated lease terms
Assess true economic motives: raising cheap off-balance sheet finance rather than operational needs.
Clear reconciliation provided between pre and post Ind AS 116.
Use management's reconciliation for accurate historical peer comparisons.
Consistent alignment between lease terms and useful life of right-of-use assets
Represents realistic economic usage without artificial tweaking.
